extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator built as an insulated styro‑foam box that contains a heat‑bed for heating and an internal fan that distributes the heated air. It is instrumented with two temperature sensors inside the box and one sensor outside. The plant is controlled by a Raspberry Pi running a bang‑bang‑style controller and a low‑level driver that issues commands to the fan and heat‑bed and reads sensor data. The system is designed to reach and maintain a target temperature regardless of the content placed inside (e.g., a bucket of cold water), making it a representative cyber‑physical system for digital‑twin research.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides (1) real‑time visualization of incubator status via a dashboard that displays live sensor readings, (2) on‑demand calibration of the thermal models (Model A and Model B) using automatically executed calibration routines, (3) anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation through a Kalman‑filter based estimator, (4) what‑if simulations by running co‑simulations that replay historical or live PT data to evaluate controller parameter changes, and (5) a self‑adaptation loop that detects behavioural changes, schedules experiments, re‑calibrates models, re‑configures the Kalman filter, runs predictive simulations and finally updates the controller settings. General DT capabilities such as monitoring, prediction, and optimisation are also supported.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation leverages a RabbitMQ message broker for synchronisation between the controller and the low‑level driver, a low‑level driver component that abstracts sensor and actuator communication, the bang‑bang controller software running on a Raspberry Pi, and an open‑source code base hosted on a GitHub repository. Calibration algorithms update model parameters, a Kalman filter algorithm provides state estimation, and two simulation models (Model A assuming full power conversion to air, Model B relaxing this assumption) are used for prediction. Collected data are stored in a time‑series store (unspecified) and visualised through a dashboard interface.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT and PT exchange data via a RabbitMQ message broker. The low‑level driver periodically reads temperature sensors and publishes the measurements to RabbitMQ; the controller subscribes to these messages and sends fan and heat‑bed commands back through the same broker. Synchronisation therefore follows the driver’s sampling period (frequency not specified) and the asynchronous delivery of messages; the documents provide no explicit latency or granularity requirements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture is a centralized, message‑oriented system. Physical hardware (heat‑bed, fan, temperature sensors) is accessed by a low‑level driver, which communicates with a controller running on a Raspberry Pi via a RabbitMQ server that acts as the integration hub. Digital‑Twin functionality is provided by software components that subscribe to RabbitMQ topics to receive sensor data and issue control commands. Orchestration is limited to the RabbitMQ broker and the host device; no additional resource‑management mechanisms are detailed.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration between the Digital Twin and the Physical Twin is performed through RabbitMQ, using its message‑passing capabilities to exchange sensor readings and control commands. The provided documents do not mention connections to external enterprise systems, other Digital Twins, or additional interoperability standards.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator digital twin contains two analytical behavioural models of the thermal dynamics, called Model A and Model B. Both models are derived from the energy‑balance equations Q = c m ΔT and E_power = V·I·Δt. Model A relies on all four simplifying assumptions (uniform air temperature, negligible wall heat storage, constant air heat capacity, and that all electrical power is converted to heat in the air). Model B relaxes Assumption 4 and therefore captures the thermal inertia of the heat‑bed. Model parameters are estimated by an on‑demand calibration routine that adjusts the model to match the temperature measurements. The data used for calibration are the three temperature sensor readings (two interior, one exterior) that are read by the low‑level driver and published as messages to the RabbitMQ communication server. Once calibrated, the models are employed by monitoring, anomaly‑detection and what‑if simulation components of the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the incubator is performed by a resistive heatbed (on/off heating element) and a centrifugal fan (on/off air circulation). Both actuators are commanded remotely by a Raspberry‑Pi‑based controller that receives set‑points and sends actuation commands through the RabbitMQ communication server. The controller implements a bang‑bang strategy with configurable lower‑limit (LL) and upper‑limit (UL) temperature thresholds, a heating duration (H) and a waiting duration (C) that together define the actuation cycle. Safety constraints are enforced by a low‑level driver that embeds a safe protocol, preventing actuation when temperature readings exceed safe limits and ensuring the device can be placed in a known safe state.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Three temperature sensors are installed: two inside the insulated styrofoam box and one outside. The low‑level driver periodically reads these sensors, uploads the measurements to the RabbitMQ server, and the controller consumes them for regulation (Document 5). The documents do not provide sensor model numbers, sampling frequencies, measurement accuracy, or resolution; only the count, placement, and data flow are described.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity rests on four simplifying assumptions: (1) uniform temperature distribution inside the box (ensured by the internal fan), (2) negligible heat accumulation in the box walls, (3) constant specific heat capacity of the air, and (4) for Model A only, that all supplied electrical power is converted to heat in the air. Model B relaxes assumption 4 to account for heat‑bed inertia. Validation is carried out by executing experiments in which the controller regulates temperature while sensor data are logged; the recorded temperature time series are compared against predictions from the calibrated models. Model parameters are fitted to the measurements using a calibration algorithm, reducing error introduced by the simplifying assumptions and measurement noise. Sources of uncertainty include the assumptions themselves and sensor noise; the calibration step mitigates these uncertainties. No additional formal verification or statistical error bounds are described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature sensors (two inside the incubator box and one outside) generate numeric temperature readings. The low‑level driver periodically reads these sensor values, applies a safe protocol, and publishes the measurements to the RabbitMQ communication server. The published messages are consumed by the Digital Twin for monitoring, calibration and visualization. Pre‑processing includes taking the average of the two internal sensor values (or selected sensors) to obtain a single box‑temperature value before transmission. No explicit sampling frequency is given; the flow is triggered continuously by the periodic reads.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin controller, running on a Raspberry Pi, receives control commands from the DT via RabbitMQ and forwards actuation messages to the low‑level driver, which drives the heat‑bed and fan. Commands correspond to the bang‑bang controller parameters (lower/upper temperature limits LL/UL, heating duration H, waiting duration C). The low‑level driver validates each command using an embedded safe protocol before applying it to the physical actuators and publishes the resulting actuator state back to RabbitMQ as feedback.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All components exchange information through a RabbitMQ message broker. The low‑level driver publishes sensor data to RabbitMQ, the controller subscribes to command topics and also publishes its control state, and both components read from the broker. RabbitMQ is described as a lightweight, easy‑to‑deploy open‑source message broker that can run on‑premises or in the cloud, providing reliable message delivery for the DT. Specific bandwidth, latency, or redundancy specifications are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital‑twin software components (low‑level driver, controller, calibration and monitoring processes) run on the edge device – a Raspberry Pi – directly attached to the incubator hardware. The RabbitMQ broker, which mediates all messaging, can be deployed either on‑premise (e.g., on the same Pi or a local server) or in a cloud environment, as RabbitMQ is described as “easy to deploy on premises and in the cloud”. No further cloud platforms, storage services, or scaling frameworks are mentioned in the source material.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The incubator DT lifecycle comprises: (1) hardware assembly of the physical twin (styrofoam box, heat‑bed, fan, temperature sensors) as described in Document 2; (2) development of two physics‑based models (Model A using all assumptions and Model B relaxing assumption 4) together with the underlying heat‑balance equations (Q = cmΔT) and model parameters (e.g., G_box) – Document 6; (3) parameter calibration using a non‑linear least‑squares solver to fit model parameters to sensor data – implied in Document 4 (on‑demand calibration); (4) integration of the DT components through a RabbitMQ communication server that exchanges messages between the low‑level driver, the controller and the DT – Document 5; (5) deployment of runtime services: continuous data acquisition from the low‑level driver, monitoring algorithms, a Kalman‑filter based state estimator and anomaly detector, and a dashboard fed from a time‑series database for real‑time visualisation – Document 4; (6) execution of what‑if co‑simulations and a self‑adaptation loop that, upon detection of plant‑behavior change, triggers experiments, re‑calibrates Model B, updates the Kalman filter and re‑configures the controller – Document 4.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The DT is engineered by first constructing the physical plant and then deriving two mathematical models (Model A and Model B) based on heat‑transfer assumptions (uniform temperature, negligible wall heat storage, constant air specific heat, and, for Model B, inclusion of heat‑bed thermal inertia) as detailed in Document 6. Model parameters are identified through a calibration step that employs a non‑linear least‑squares optimisation to minimise the error between measured sensor data and model predictions (Section 3.3, referenced in Document 4). The software architecture uses a RabbitMQ message broker for reliable publish/subscribe communication between the low‑level driver, the controller running on a Raspberry Pi, and the DT components (Document 5). Validation is performed experimentally: two sets of experiments are conducted to compare the predictive performance of Model A versus Model B (Figure 13, Document 4). Evolution of the DT is managed by “on‑demand calibration”, which can be re‑run automatically when new data are available, and by the self‑adaptation loop that iteratively updates the model, state estimator and controller based on detected anomalies (Document 4). No explicit quality‑assurance or version‑control procedures are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT provides decision support through several mechanisms. Sensor readings from the three temperature sensors are streamed via the low‑level driver to the RabbitMQ server and stored in a time‑series database; a dashboard visualises the current incubator temperature in real time (Document 4). A Kalman filter built on Model B fuses the noisy temperature measurements to estimate the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and to detect anomalies in plant behaviour (Document 4). When an anomaly is flagged, the self‑adaptation loop initiates a sequence of actions: it schedules a data‑gathering experiment, recalibrates Model B with the new data, re‑configures the Kalman filter, runs what‑if co‑simulations to evaluate alternative controller settings, and finally updates the controller parameters accordingly (Document 4). These insights—state estimates, anomaly alerts, and simulation‑based optimisation recommendations—are presented to operators through the dashboard and used to re‑configure the controller automatically.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The incubator Digital Twin incorporates several safety and security measures described in the documentation. The low‑level driver includes a built‑in safe protocol that continuously checks temperature readings and command messages before issuing actuation commands, ensuring the physical twin remains in a safe state. Communication between the controller, driver, and other components is mediated by a RabbitMQ message broker, which is characterized as a mature, lightweight, and widely deployed open‑source server, providing reliable message delivery for the DT. The controller implements a bang‑bang control strategy with defined lower and upper temperature limits (LL, UL), a heating duration (H) and a waiting duration (C) to prevent temperature overshoot, representing a safety‑oriented control logic. Additionally, the DT employs an anomaly‑detection and heat‑bed state‑estimation module that runs a Kalman filter on Model B together with the measured sensor data to estimate the non‑measured heat‑bed temperature and detect deviations, supporting fault detection and safety monitoring.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to

the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors

detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical

and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature

are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
